questions.question: A parliamentary system of government is one in which
questions.option_1: All political parties in the parliament are represented in the government
questions.option_2: The government is responsible to the Parliament and can be removed by it
questions.option_3: The government is elected by the people and can be removed by them
questions.option_4: The government is chosen by the parliament but cannot be removed by it before the completion of a fixed term
questions.solution:
The bedrock of a Parliamentary System is the fusion of powers between the executive and the legislature. In this system:

  • Accountability: The Executive (Council of Ministers) is drawn from and remains accountable to the Legislature.
  • Removal: The government stays in power only as long as it enjoys the confidence of the lower house (Lok Sabha in India). If a 'No-Confidence Motion' is passed, the government is obliged to resign.
  • Contrast: This differs from a Presidential system where the executive is independent of the legislature and usually has a fixed term.

questions.question: In the context of India which one of the following is the characteristic appropriate for Bureaucracy?
questions.option_1: An agency for widening the scope of parliamentary democracy
questions.option_2: An agency for strengthening the structure of federalism
questions.option_3: An agency for facilitating political stability and economic growth
questions.option_4: An agency for the implementation of public policy
questions.solution:
In a democratic setup like India, the Bureaucracy (Civil Services) acts as the permanent executive. Its primary role is:

  • Implementation: While the political executive (Ministers) decides on policies, the bureaucracy is the agency responsible for the execution and implementation of these public policies and laws.
  • Neutrality: It is expected to remain politically neutral and provide continuity in administration regardless of changes in the governing party.

questions.question: Democracy's superior virtue lies in the fact that it calls into activity
questions.option_1: The intelligence and character of ordinary men and women.
questions.option_2: The methods for strengthening executive leadership
questions.option_3: A superior individual with dynamism and vision.
questions.option_4: A band of dedicated party workers.
questions.solution:
The "superior virtue" of democracy, as argued by many political theorists (like J.S. Mill), is its developmental aspect. Unlike other forms of government that treat people as passive subjects, democracy:

  • Engagement: Requires citizens to participate in decision-making, which stimulates their intelligence.
  • Responsibility: Encourages a sense of civic duty and moral character by giving ordinary men and women a stake in how they are governed.

questions.question: The main advantage of the parliamentary form of government is that
questions.option_1: the executive and legislature work independently
questions.option_2: it provides continuity of policy and is more efficient.
questions.option_3: the executive remains responsible to the legislature
questions.option_4: the head of the government cannot be changed without election.
questions.solution: The most significant advantage of the parliamentary form of government is the continuous accountability of the executive. Since the ministers are members of the parliament, they are subject to daily scrutiny through questions, debates, and motions. This ensures that the executive remains responsible to the elected representatives of the people at all times.

questions.question: Out of the following statements, choose the one that brings out the principle underlying the Cabinet form of Government:
questions.option_1: An arrangement for minimizing the criticism against the Government whose responsibilities are complex and hard to carry out to the satisfaction of all.
questions.option_2: A mechanism for speeding up the activities of the Government whose responsibilities are increasing day by day.
questions.option_3: A mechanism of parliamentary democracy for ensuring collective responsibility of the Government to the people.
questions.option_4: A device for strengthening the hands of the head of the Government whose hold over the people is in a state of decline.
questions.solution:
The Cabinet form of government, often called 'Responsible Government', hinges on the principle of Collective Responsibility (Article 75 in India):

  • Unity: The Cabinet acts as a single unit. If the Lok Sabha passes a motion of no-confidence against one minister or the cabinet as a whole, the entire government falls.
  • Accountability: This mechanism ensures the government is collectively answerable to the Parliament, which represents the will of the people.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a feature of Indian federalism?
questions.option_1: There is an independent judiciary in India.
questions.option_2: Powers have been clearly divided between the Centre and the States.
questions.option_3: The federating units have been given unequal representation in the Rajya Sabha.
questions.option_4: It is the result of an agreement among the federating units.
questions.solution:
Indian federalism is often described as 'sui generis' or 'Quasi-federal'. Its features include:

  • Non-Agreement: Unlike the USA, the Indian federation is not the result of an agreement among sovereign states. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar noted that the Indian Union is 'indestructible,' but the states are not.
  • Independent Judiciary: Essential for resolving disputes between the Centre and States.
  • Division of Powers: Clearly defined through the Union, State, and Concurrent lists.
  • Representation: Seats in the Rajya Sabha are allocated based on population, leading to unequal representation among states (unlike the US Senate).

questions.question: There is a Parliamentary System of Government in India because the
questions.option_1: Lok Sabha is elected directly by the people
questions.option_4: Council of Ministers is responsible to the Lok Sabha
questions.solution: The defining characteristic that makes India a Parliamentary System (as opposed to a Presidential one) is that the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha. While the election of the Lok Sabha and the power to amend the Constitution are parts of Indian democracy, they do not specifically define the 'Parliamentary' nature of the executive.

questions.question: The Parliament can make any law for whole or any part of India for implementing international treaties
questions.option_1: With the consent of all the States
questions.option_2: With the consent of the majority of States
questions.option_3: With the consent of the States concerned
questions.option_4: Without the consent of any State
questions.solution: Under Article 253 of the Constitution, the Parliament has the power to make laws for the whole or any part of India to implement any international treaty, agreement, or convention. This power allows the Parliament to legislate even on subjects mentioned in the State List without the consent of any State, ensuring that India can meet its international obligations.

questions.question:
In the context of India, which of the following principles is/are implied institutionally in the parliamentary government?

  1. Members of the Cabinet are Members of the Parliament.
  2. Ministers hold the office till they enjoy confidence in the Parliament.
  3. The Cabinet is headed by the Head of the State.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
questions.solution:
Institutional principles of the Indian Parliamentary system include:

  • Dual Membership: A minister must be a member of the Parliament (either Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha). If they are not, they must become one within six months.
  • Confidence of Parliament: Ministers hold office as long as they enjoy the majority support (confidence) in the Lok Sabha.
  • Head of State: The Cabinet is headed by the Prime Minister (Head of Government), not the President (Head of State). The President is the nominal executive.

questions.question:
With reference to Union Government, consider the following statements:

  1. The Constitution of India provides that all Cabinet Ministers shall be compulsorily the sitting members of Lok Sabha only
  2. The Union Cabinet Secretariat operates under the direction of the Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Clarifying the structure of the Union Government:

  • Ministerial Rank: Cabinet Ministers can be members of either the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha. There is no requirement for them to belong only to the Lok Sabha.
  • Cabinet Secretariat: It functions directly under the Prime Minister, not the Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs. The administrative head of the Secretariat is the Cabinet Secretary.

questions.question: The Indian parliamentary system is different from the British parliamentary system in that India has
questions.option_1: Both a real and a nominal executive
questions.solution:
While India adopted the British Westminister model, there are key differences:

  • Judicial Review: In India, the Constitution is supreme, and the Judiciary can declare a law passed by Parliament as unconstitutional. In the UK, the Parliament is sovereign, and its acts generally cannot be struck down by courts.
  • Republic vs. Monarchy: India has an elected Head of State (President), whereas the UK has a hereditary Monarch.
  • Commonalities: Both systems share bicameralism, collective responsibility, and the real/nominal executive split.
